raw_text.question:
Imagine you're organizing a tournament with teams from different cities, and each match can end in one of two outcomes: a win for the home team (marked with red) or a win for the visiting team (marked with blue). What is the minimum number of teams you need to invite to ensure that, no matter the outcomes, you will have either four teams where each team has won against each of the other three teams at home (a red quartet) or four teams where each has lost to each of the other three teams while visiting (a blue quartet)?

[Document 1]
\section{Infinite Ramsey's Theorem implies Finite Ramsey's Theorem}
Tags: Ramsey Theory

\begin{theorem}
:$\forall l, n, r \in \N: \exists m \in \N: m \to \left({l}\right)_r^n$
where $\alpha \to \left({\beta}\right)^n_r$ means that:
:for any assignment of $r$-colors to the $n$-subsets of $\alpha$
::there is a particular color $\gamma$ and a subset $X$ of $\alpha$ of size $\beta$ such that all $n$-subsets of $X$ are $\gamma$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
{{AimForCont}} there is a $l$ such that:
:$\forall m \in \N: m \nrightarrow \left({l}\right)_r^n$
Let $\hat{K_i}$ denote a hypergraph on $i$ vertices where all possible $n$-subsets of the vertices are the hyperedges.
Let $G$ be a hypergraph with vertices $V = \left\{ {v_i: i \in \N}\right\}$.
Let the hyperedges of $G <$ be enumerated by:
:$E = \left\{ {E_i: E_i \subset \N, \left\vert{E_i}\right\vert = n}\right\}$
{{explain|What does $G <$ mean?}}
We construct a (rooted) tree $T$ as follows:
$(1): \quad$ First introduce a root node $r t$.
$(2): \quad$ Each node is allowed to have at most $r<$ children which correspond to the $r$-colors, subject to it satisfying the criteria:
{{explain|What does $r <$ mean?}}
:::A child is always labeled by one among the $r$-colors.
:::Let the colors be denoted $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_r$.
$(3): \quad$ A child $c_i$ is '''permitted''' {{iff}} its introduction creates a path of some finite length $k$ starting from the root.
:::So, if the hyperedges $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_k$ are colored by the colors used in the path in the same order, then the corresponding subgraph of $G$ does not contain a monochromatic $\hat{K_l}$.
:::For example: if the introduction of a child $c_i$ creates the $k$ length path $r t, c_a, c_b, \ldots, c_i$ and the hyperedges $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_k$ when colored $c_a, c_b, \ldots, c_i$ do not contain a monochromatic $\hat{K_l}$, the child $c_i$ is permitted to be added to $T$.
Note that for all $m$, there always exists a coloring of $\hat{K_m}$ such that no monochromatic $\hat{K_l}$ exists within.
Thus the situation that a child cannot be added to any vertex at a given level $k$ cannot arise.
For we can always take a coloring of $\hat{K_{k+n}}$ containing no monochromatic $\hat{K_l}$.
Since any $k$ hyperedges in it would yield a sequence of colors already existing in $T$, we know which vertex to add the child to.
We give the child the color corresponding to any other edge.
Hence we can forever keep adding children and so $T$ is infinite.
It is also obvious that each level $k$ of $T$ has at most $r^k<$ vertices.
So each level is finite.
By König's Tree Lemma there will be an infinite path $P$ in $T$.
$P$ provides a $r$-coloring of $G$ that contains no monochromatic $\hat{K_i}$.
Hence $P$ contains no monochromatic infinite hypergraph.
This contradicts the Infinite Ramsey's Theorem.
The result follows by Proof by Contradiction.
{{qed}}
Category:Ramsey Theory
\end{proof}

\section{Omitting Types Theorem}
Tags: Named Theorems, Model Theory

\begin{theorem}
Let $\LL$ be the language of predicate logic with a countable signature.
Let $T$ be an $\LL$-theory.
Let $\set {p_i: i \in \N}$ be a countable set of non-isolated $n$-types of $T$.
There is a countable $\LL$-structure $\MM$ such that $\MM \models T$ and $\MM$ omits each $p_i$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
This proof is done by constructing a model using a method known as a Henkin construction.
This results in a model all of whose elements are the interpretations of constant symbols from some language.
The construction for this proof in particular is done so that the theory this model satisfies asserts that each tuple of constants fails to satisfy at least one $\phi$ from each type $p_i$.
As a result, the model we construct will not realize any of the types.
We will prove the theorem for the special case where we only omit one type.
That is, assume $\set {p_i: i \in \N}$ is $\set p$ for some $p$.
Comments on the general case will be made after the proof.
Our goal is to eventually be able to apply Maximal Finitely Satisfiable Theory with Witness Property is Satisfiable to a suitable $\LL^*$-theory $T^*$, where $\LL^*$ contains $\LL$ and $T^*$ contains $T$.
Let $\LL^*$ be $\LL \cup \set {c_i : i \in \N}$ where each $c_i$ is a new constant symbol.
Note that $\LL^*$ is still countable.
We will recursively construct $T^*$ by adding a sentence $\theta_k$ at each stage.
In order to ensure that it has the necessary properties, we will construct it in stages, each of which has three steps.
The goal of the first step of each stage is ensure that $T^*$ will be maximal upon completion of the definition.
The goal of the second step of each stage is to ensure that $T^*$ will satisfy the witness property.
The goal of the third step of each stage is to ensure that $p$ is not realized by any tuple of constants.
Since $\LL^*$ is countable, the set of $\LL$-formulas is countable, and hence in particular, the $\LL^*$-sentences can be listed as $\phi_0, \phi_1, \ldots$
Similarly, since $\LL^*$ is countable, the set of $n$-tuples of its constant symbols can be listed as $\bar d_0, \bar d_1, \dots$
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Definition:Bozo Bit]
The concept of '''bozo bit''' was adopted by the computer industry in the following manner.
The typical stereotype of a worker in the computer software industry is that he (usually male) is limited in his ability to interact skilfully with his colleagues.
In a team of people, there are bound to be clashes of personality. During the course of interactions with one of your colleagues, one of them sticks in your mind for making unhelpful suggestions, or crafting inept designs, or otherwise demonstrating a modicum of stupidity that is likely to become a hindrance to the task in progress.
What can happen is for a team leader, or someone else whose input is influential, to register this person as a bozo, and prejudge every contribution of that person to be worthless. When the bozo is speaking, the listener mentally tunes out the sound of his voice and is instead thinking of something completely different.
Such behaviour is known as '''flipping the bozo bit''' on that person.
\end{definition}

[Document 4]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Ramsey Number]
Ramsey's Theorem states that in any coloring of the edges of a sufficiently large complete graph, one will find monochromatic complete subgraphs.
More precisely, for any given number of colors $c$, and any given integers $n_1, \ldots, n_c$, there is a number $\map R {n_1, \ldots, n_c}$ such that:
:if the edges of a complete graph of order $\map R {n_1, \ldots, n_c}$ are colored with $c$ different colours, then for some $i$ between $1$ and $c$, it must contain a complete subgraph of order $n_i$ whose edges are all color $i$.
This number $\map R {n_1, \ldots, n_c}$ is called the '''Ramsey number''' for $n_1, \ldots, n_c$.
{{NamedforDef|Frank Plumpton Ramsey|cat = Ramsey}}
Category:Definitions/Ramsey Theory
\end{definition}

[Document 5]
\section{Ramsey's Theorem}
Tags: Ramsey Theory, Named Theorems, Combinatorics

\begin{theorem}
In any coloring of the edges of a sufficiently large complete graph, one will find monochromatic complete subgraphs.
For 2 colors, Ramsey's theorem states that for any pair of positive integers $\tuple {r, s}$, there exists a least positive integer $\map R {r, s}$ such that for any complete graph on $\map R {r, s}$ vertices, whose edges are colored red or blue, there exists either a complete subgraph on $r$ vertices which is entirely red, or a complete subgraph on $s$ vertices which is entirely blue.
More generally, for any given number of colors $c$, and any given integers $n_1, \ldots, n_c$, there is a number $\map R {n_1, \ldots, n_c}$ such that:
:if the edges of a complete graph of order $\map R {n_1, \ldots, n_c}$ are colored with $c$ different colours, then for some $i$ between $1$ and $c$, it must contain a complete subgraph of order $n_i$ whose edges are all color $i$.
This number $\map R {n_1, \ldots, n_c}$ is called the Ramsey number for $n_1, \ldots, n_c$.
The special case above has $c = 2$ (and $n_1 = r$ and $n_2 = s$).
Here $\map R {r, s}$ signifies an integer that depends on both $r$ and $s$. It is understood to represent the smallest integer for which the theorem holds.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
First we prove the theorem for the 2-color case, by induction on $r + s$.
It is clear from the definition that
:$\forall n \in \N: \map R {n, 1} = \map R {1, n} = 1$
because the complete graph on one node has no edges.
This is the base case.
We prove that $R \left({r, s}\right)$ exists by finding an explicit bound for it.
By the inductive hypothesis, $\map R {r - 1, s}$ and $\map R {r, s - 1}$ exist.
\end{proof}

So, for team A, if it hosted B, it lost to B at home. If it hosted C, lost to C at home. If it hosted D, lost to D at home. But if it visited E, it won, but in the quartet, only B,C,D are considered.

In a set of four teams, for team A, it plays three matches: against B,C,D.

Suppose in these, it hosted some and visited some.

If it hosted B, and since away won, B won, so A lost at home to B.

If it visited C, and away won, so A won while visiting C.

So, for team A, it lost to B at home (if it hosted B), and won at C's home (if it visited C).

The condition "each has lost to each of the other three teams while visiting" — for team A, it should have lost to B while visiting B, lost to C while visiting C, lost to D while visiting D.

But if it visited B, and lost, then it lost while visiting B.

But for this to happen, A must have visited B, visited C, and visited D. That is, A was away in all its matches.

Similarly for all other teams, each must have been away in all their matches within the quartet.

But if A is away against B, that means B is home. If A is away against C, C is home. If A is away against D, D is home.

Now for team B, it must have visited A, but if A is away against B, then B is home, so B is not away against A; in fact, B is home against A. But for B to lose to A while visiting, it must have visited A, which would require that in the match with A, B was away, but we have B was home, contradiction.

So again, impossibility.

Therefore, the only reasonable interpretation is that for the red quartet, all matches in the four-team subset are won by the home team, and for the blue quartet, all matches are won by the away team.

And the verbal description is meant to convey that, but it's poorly worded.

In the red quartet, "each team has won against each of the other three teams at home" is not accurate because a team may not have hosted all others; it may have won the ones it hosted, but lost the ones it visited, but in a red quartet, if it visited and the home team won, it lost, so it didn't win against that team at home or at all.

Perhaps it's "each team that was home won its game", but that's trivial.

I think we have to go with the intended meaning: a red quartet is a set of four teams such that in every match between them, the home team won. Similarly, a blue quartet is a set where in every match, the away team won.

And this is equivalent to the subgraph being monochromatic if we define the color based on who won: red for home win, blue for away win.

And as we saw earlier, the adversary can make each edge red or blue independently by choosing who is home and who wins.

For example, to make an edge red, set A home and A wins; to make it blue, set A home and B wins (so away wins).

So yes, the adversary can control the color of each edge freely.

Therefore, the problem reduces to finding the smallest n such as that any 2-coloring of the edges of K_n contains a monochromatic K4.

Which is R(4,4) = 18.

So the answer should be 18.

But to confirm, is R(4,4) indeed 18?

I recall that R(3,3)=6, R(4,3)=9, R(4,4)=18.

Yes, and it's known that R(4,4)=18.

For example, the unique coloring without monochromatic K4 is the Paley graph or something, but for 17 vertices, there is a 2-coloring without monochromatic K4.

So, with 17 teams, it's possible to assign home teams and winners such that no four teams have all home wins or all away wins in their mutual matches.

With 18 teams, it's impossible to avoid.

Therefore, the minimum number is 18.

So, the answer is 18.
</think>
The problem requires finding the minimum number of teams to ensure that, regardless of the outcomes of the matches and the assignment of home teams, there is either a red quartet or a blue quartet. A red quartet is a set of four teams where, in every match between them, the home team wins (i.e., all matches are colored red). A blue quartet is a set of four teams where, in every match between them, the away team wins (i.e., all matches are colored blue).

The assignment of the home team for each match and the outcome of each match are chosen adversarially. For any pair of teams, the adversary can choose which team is home and then choose the winner, allowing the adversary to control the color (red or blue) of each edge independently. Specifically:
- To color an edge red (home win), the adversary designates one team as home and has that team win.
- To color an edge blue (away win), the adversary designates one team as home and has the away team win.

Thus, the adversary can assign any 2-coloring (red or blue) to the edges of the complete graph formed by the teams. The problem then reduces to finding the smallest number of vertices (teams) such that any 2-coloring of the edges of the complete graph \(K_n\) contains a monochromatic complete subgraph \(K_4\) (i.e., all edges red or all edges blue).

This is the definition of the Ramsey number \(R(4,4)\), which is the smallest integer \(n\) such that every 2-coloring of the edges of \(K_n\) contains a monochromatic \(K_4\). It is known that \(R(4,4) = 18\).

Therefore, with 18 teams, no matter how the home teams are assigned and no matter the outcomes of the matches, there will always be either a red quartet or a blue quartet. With 17 teams, it is possible to assign home teams and outcomes such that no such quartet exists, as demonstrated by known colorings of \(K_{17}\) without a monochromatic \(K_4\).

Thus, the minimum number of teams required is 18.

\boxed{18}
